prompt: What does the phrasal verb 'give up' mean in 'Don't give up on your dreams'?

options.because: 'Give up' means to stop trying or to abandon an effort. It is a separable phrasal verb: 'give up smoking' or 'give it up'.
'Give' alone means to present something. 'Give up' is an idiomatic phrasal verb with a completely different meaning.
'Give up' means to stop/quit. 'Go up' means to increase or rise.
'Give back' means to return. 'Give up' means to quit or surrender.

explanation: Phrasal verbs combine a verb + particle (preposition or adverb) to create a new idiomatic meaning: give up (quit), look up (search/look toward), run into (meet unexpectedly), turn down (refuse). The meaning is often not predictable from the parts.

prompt: Expand the contraction to its full form.
template: 'I'm' is a contraction of {{blank_1}}, and 'won't' is a contraction of {{blank_2}}.
explanation: Common contractions: I'm (I am), you're (you are), he's (he is/has), it's (it is/has), we'll (we will), won't (will not — note the spelling change), can't (cannot), don't (do not), didn't (did not).

prompt: Match each phrasal verb to its meaning.
explanation: Phrasal verbs are very common in spoken and informal English. They can be separable (put it off / put off the meeting) or inseparable (run out of money — you cannot separate these). Learning them in context is most effective.
